critic_raw:
The core methodological problem is the stratified random patient-level split for hospital readmission prediction. Patients have multiple admissions over the five-year observation window, and a random split will place different admissions from the same patient in training and test sets. This creates patient-level leakage: the model observes a patient's clinical history in training and then predicts their readmission from a later admission in the test set, having already seen that patient's response to prior treatment. This inflates performance for the deep model specifically (which can encode patient identity implicitly through features like prior admission count and comorbidity patterns) more than for logistic regression. An admission-level random split with patient-level holdout is the minimum requirement.

Second, the comparison is between gradient-boosted trees with SDOH features versus logistic regression with only clinical features. This confounds two distinct sources of variation: model family (GBT vs logistic regression) and feature set (clinical + SDOH vs clinical only). If GBT with SDOH outperforms logistic regression with clinical features, it is impossible to attribute the gain to SDOH rather than GBT's capacity to model nonlinear interactions in the clinical features alone. To isolate the SDOH contribution, the design requires at minimum a GBT model trained on clinical features only. The design's stated goal is to test 'incremental value of SDOH factors' but the experiment as designed does not isolate that increment.

Third, SDOH variables (income, education, housing status) exhibit systematic missingness that is not missing at random — patients who lack housing or have low income are less likely to have these variables recorded in EHRs. If imputation is applied without accounting for this non-random missingness pattern, the imputed values will systematically underestimate SDOH risk in exactly the population where SDOH is most predictive. The design mentions imputation on the training set but does not address the mechanism of missingness.

Fourth, AUC-ROC as the primary metric measures rank ordering but not calibration. For hospital readmission prediction used to allocate follow-up resources, the model's probability outputs need to be calibrated to support decision thresholds. A well-calibrated model with lower AUC may be more clinically useful than a poorly calibrated model with higher AUC. The design mentions calibration slope as a concern only implicitly, not as a reported metric.

Fifth, stratifying analysis across demographic subgroups for confound control is described as a check, but if SDOH features are correlated with demographic variables (which they are, by construction), then subgroup analysis will not cleanly separate SDOH effects from demographic effects — it will merely redistribute the confounding across subgroup cells.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level random split with multiple admissions per patient allows the model to observe a patient's full clinical trajectory in training and predict their later admission in the test set, inflating GBT performance through implicit patient identity leakage.

The comparison confounds model family (GBT vs. logistic regression) with feature set (clinical + SDOH vs. clinical only), making it impossible to attribute performance gains to SDOH rather than GBT's nonlinear capacity on clinical features alone; a clinical-only GBT control is required.

SDOH variables have non-random missingness — low-income, unstably housed patients are less likely to have SDOH recorded — so imputation without modeling the missingness mechanism will systematically underestimate SDOH risk precisely in the population where it is most predictive.

AUC-ROC measures rank ordering but not calibration; for resource allocation decisions requiring probability thresholds, a poorly calibrated high-AUC model may be clinically inferior to a well-calibrated lower-AUC model, and calibration is not a reported metric.

Subgroup analysis by demographics cannot cleanly separate SDOH effects from demographic effects because SDOH variables (income, housing) are structurally correlated with demographic variables, redistributing rather than eliminating the confound.
all_issues_adjudicated: Patient-level random split with multiple admissions per patient allows the model to observe a patient's full clinical trajectory in training and predict their later admission in the test set, inflating GBT performance through implicit patient identity leakage.

The comparison confounds model family (GBT vs. logistic regression) with feature set (clinical + SDOH vs. clinical only), making it impossible to attribute performance gains to SDOH rather than GBT's nonlinear capacity on clinical features alone; a clinical-only GBT control is required.

SDOH variables have non-random missingness — low-income, unstably housed patients are less likely to have SDOH recorded — so imputation without modeling the missingness mechanism will systematically underestimate SDOH risk precisely in the population where it is most predictive.

AUC-ROC measures rank ordering but not calibration; for resource allocation decisions requiring probability thresholds, a poorly calibrated high-AUC model may be clinically inferior to a well-calibrated lower-AUC model, and calibration is not a reported metric.

Subgroup analysis by demographics cannot cleanly separate SDOH effects from demographic effects because SDOH variables (income, housing) are structurally correlated with demographic variables, redistributing rather than eliminating the confound.
